critic_raw:
**Issue 1: Structured data preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting.**
Section 3 states that numerical feature standardization uses 'dataset-wide statistics (mean and standard deviation)' — explicitly fit on the entire dataset rather than on training data only. This is data leakage. The note in the same section that text and image preprocessing use training-only statistics makes the inconsistency more puzzling: the design correctly handles image normalization (training images only) and text preprocessing but leaks training statistics for structured features. This asymmetric leakage introduces a bias toward the structured modality's contribution in the test set.

**Issue 2: Frozen ImageNet DenseNet-121 for echocardiogram images is a questionable and untested assumption.**
The design uses a frozen DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet to produce 1024-dimensional feature vectors from echocardiogram images. Echocardiogram images are grayscale, temporally dynamic, and structurally unlike photographic scenes — low-level ImageNet filters respond to natural image textures and color gradients that are irrelevant to cardiac ultrasonography. Freezing the weights means the model cannot adapt to echocardiographic statistics. The design justifies freezing to 'avoid encoding label information into embeddings' — but this rationale applies to fine-tuning on readmission labels, not to domain adaptation of the visual encoder. Using a cardiac-domain pretrained model (e.g., EchoNet) or at least reporting what proportion of variance in readmission is explained by the frozen features would validate this choice. As designed, the image branch may contribute near-zero informative signal while adding dimensionality.

**Issue 3: Patient-level data integrity enforcement contradicts the fixed modality availability problem.**
Section 2 enforces patient-level split integrity (all admissions for a patient in one split), which is correct. However, Section 7 includes a confound control for 'documentation completeness: performance for complete vs. partial imaging availability.' This implies that not all 20,000 records have complete echocardiogram images. If imaging availability is systematically correlated with patient severity (sicker patients are more likely to receive echo imaging), then patients with complete imaging form a selected subpopulation — and the multi-modal model is evaluated on a different effective population than the text-only baseline when both are tested on the same test set (since the multi-modal model must drop or impute missing images). The design does not describe how records with missing echocardiograms are handled in the multi-modal model.

**Issue 4: The hypothesis requires showing multi-modal outperforms text-only, but the two models differ on three dimensions simultaneously.**
The multi-modal model combines text (BioBERT + BiLSTM), imaging (DenseNet-121 + GAP), and structured features, while the baseline uses text only (BioBERT + BiLSTM + dense layers). The multi-modal model thus has additional modalities (imaging, structured features) and additional architectural complexity (more parameters, additional branches, different pooling paths). If the multi-modal model outperforms, the gain cannot be attributed to the additional modality alone — it could reflect architectural capacity, the structured features, or both. A fair test of the multi-modal hypothesis requires holding all other factors constant.

**Issue 5: 20 random hyperparameter trials is statistically insufficient for a multi-branch architecture.**
The multi-modal model has LSTM units, learning rate, dropout, and potentially image branch and structured branch hyperparameters across three parallel branches. With 20 random trials, the probability of finding a near-optimal configuration in a high-dimensional hyperparameter space is low. The baseline (text-only, fewer hyperparameters) may be more reliably tuned with the same budget, systematically disadvantaging the multi-modal model. The design should report the dimensionality of the hyperparameter space being searched for each model and justify 20 trials as sufficient.
defender_raw:
This design tests whether adding imaging (echocardiogram) and structured data to clinical notes improves 30-day readmission prediction in heart failure patients. This is one of the methodologically stronger designs in the batch.

Preprocessing soundness — one flaw: The design correctly specifies that image normalization statistics are computed from training images only. However, it also states that structured data preprocessing uses 'dataset-wide statistics' for normalization. This is a leakage error for the structured features, though it is inconsistent with the correctly handled image preprocessing. The text features use frozen pretrained embeddings (no training-data-dependent parameters needed). So the leakage is limited to structured features, not all modalities.

Split strategy — genuinely strong: This is one of the few designs in this batch that uses a proper temporal split: months 1-36 training, 37-48 validation, 49-60 test. Patient-level integrity is explicitly enforced — if a patient has multiple admissions, all go to a single split. This prevents both temporal leakage and patient-level leakage. The rationale ('temporal splitting prevents a model from knowing future outcomes') is correct and well-stated.

Baseline construction is well-specified: the clinical notes-only unimodal model uses the same pretrained transformer (BioBERT), same LSTM architecture, same training protocol. The only difference is the absence of image and structured branches. This correctly isolates the added value of imaging and structured data, not differences in text modeling capacity.

Using frozen pretrained models (BioBERT, DenseNet-121) without fine-tuning on training labels is a sound choice for a PoC where the goal is to test modality value, not architecture optimization. It prevents the risk of label information being encoded into embeddings via fine-tuning on a small dataset.

AUROC as the primary metric is appropriate for this imbalanced binary classification problem. AUPRC is included as a secondary metric specifically for imbalance sensitivity, and sensitivity at 90% specificity is the operationally relevant threshold metric.

Subgroup consistency requirement — strong design choice: The design states 'the hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUROC than the baseline consistently across all subgroups.' This is a stricter criterion than simple overall AUROC comparison and guards against gains driven by spurious patterns in a specific demographic or temporal window.

The design is among the most rigorous in this batch. The structured feature leakage is the primary concession; everything else is soundly motivated.
all_issues_raised: Structured feature standardization explicitly uses dataset-wide statistics before splitting, leaking test-set distribution information — while image and text preprocessing correctly use training-only statistics, creating an asymmetric leakage.

Frozen DenseNet-121 (ImageNet weights) applied to echocardiogram images is not validated as a useful feature extractor for this domain; echocardiography images differ fundamentally from natural photos, and frozen filters may produce near-zero informative signal.

The handling of patients with missing echocardiogram images is not specified — if imaging availability is correlated with severity, the multi-modal model evaluates on a selected subpopulation, making the comparison with the text-only baseline on the same test set asymmetric.

The multi-modal model differs from the baseline on three dimensions simultaneously (additional modalities, structured features, architectural branches), so any observed performance difference cannot be attributed specifically to the multi-modal modality contribution.

20 random hyperparameter trials is statistically inadequate for a high-dimensional multi-branch architecture search space, and the baseline with fewer hyperparameters may be more reliably tuned with the same budget.
all_issues_adjudicated: Structured feature standardization explicitly uses dataset-wide statistics before splitting, leaking test-set distribution information — while image and text preprocessing correctly use training-only statistics, creating an asymmetric leakage.

Frozen DenseNet-121 (ImageNet weights) applied to echocardiogram images is not validated as a useful feature extractor for this domain; echocardiography images differ fundamentally from natural photos, and frozen filters may produce near-zero informative signal.

The handling of patients with missing echocardiogram images is not specified — if imaging availability is correlated with severity, the multi-modal model evaluates on a selected subpopulation, making the comparison with the text-only baseline on the same test set asymmetric.

The multi-modal model differs from the baseline on three dimensions simultaneously (additional modalities, structured features, architectural branches), so any observed performance difference cannot be attributed specifically to the multi-modal modality contribution.
